slides.title: A Function That Calls Itself
slides.body: **Recursion** is when a function calls *itself* to solve a problem. Think of nesting dolls — each one contains a smaller version of the same thing, all the way down to the tiniest doll.
slides.tip: Recursion isn't magic — it's just a function calling itself with a simpler input each time.

slides.title: Two Rules: Stop and Shrink
slides.body: Every recursive function needs a **base case** (when to stop) and a **recursive case** (call itself with something smaller). Without the base case, it runs forever.
slides.code:
def countdown(n):
    if n <= 0:        # base case
        print("Blast off!")
    else:             # recursive case
        print(n)
        countdown(n - 1)

countdown(3)

slides.title: Classic Example: Factorial
slides.body: `5!` means `5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1`. Notice that `5! = 5 × 4!` — the problem contains a **smaller copy of itself**, making it a perfect fit for recursion.
slides.code:
def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1:           # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)  # recursive case

print(factorial(5))
slides.tip: factorial(5) waits for factorial(4), which waits for factorial(3)... then answers bubble back up.

slides.title: The Call Stack Winds & Unwinds
slides.body: Python tracks each call on a **call stack**. Calls pile up until the base case is hit, then results bubble back up through each waiting call — like stacked plates being removed one by one.
slides.code:
# factorial(3) traces like this:
# factorial(3) -> 3 * factorial(2)
#   factorial(2) -> 2 * factorial(1)
#     factorial(1) -> 1  (base case!)
# unwinds: 2*1=2, then 3*2=6
print(factorial(3))

slides.title: Missing Base Case = RecursionError
slides.body: If your function never reaches its base case, Python will raise a `RecursionError` after ~**1000 calls**. This safety limit protects your computer from runaway recursion.
slides.tip: Always check: does your recursive case actually move *closer* to the base case each time?

slides.title: You Get Recursion Now!
slides.body: Every recursive function has a **base case** to stop and a **recursive case** to shrink the problem. Use recursion when a problem naturally breaks into smaller copies of itself — like trees, folders, or math sequences.
slides.tip: When in doubt, write the base case first — it's the anchor that makes everything else work.
